title: Loading, empty, success and failure states

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Loading, empty, success and failure states; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Loading, empty, success and failure states
sources.claim: Data UI explicitly represents idle, loading, success, empty, stale, and error states rather than treating data as one nullable value.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Data UI explicitly represents idle, loading, success, empty, stale, and error states rather than treating data as one nullable value.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A course list distinguishes loading skeleton, empty catalog, loaded rows, and retryable server error. Your goal is to render and test every observable remote-data state. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Cleanup and race prevention; javascript network fetch; Conditional rendering. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Data UI explicitly represents idle, loading, success, empty, stale, and error states rather than treating data as one nullable value. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Previous data, background refresh, partial results, and permission failures can overlap these labels. This draft therefore rejects: An empty array during loading is equivalent to a confirmed empty result.

includes: Model remote data as explicit states rather than ambiguous null checks.
Render and test every observable remote-data state.
excludes: Previous data, background refresh, partial results, and permission failures can overlap these labels.

outcomes: Render and test every observable remote-data state.

examples.title: Loading, empty, success and failure states: concrete case

walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A course list distinguishes loading skeleton, empty catalog, loaded rows, and retryable server error.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Track loading and outcome separately so unknown data is not misrepresented as a confirmed empty state.
